content.text: Domain Stewardship for Claude Knowledge Bases

A Claude knowledge base is only as trustworthy as the governance process that controls what enters it. Domain stewards — the subject-matter experts who own specific data assets — are the gatekeepers for every document, database record, and tool output that can enter Claude's retrieval corpus. Without active stewardship, knowledge bases accumulate stale, misclassified, or over-privileged content that generates hallucinations, data leaks, and compliance exposure.

attrs.alt: Domain steward lifecycle for Claude knowledge bases: Assess, Approve, Ingest, Monitor, Retire

content.text: The five-phase lifecycle above captures a domain steward's recurring responsibilities. Each phase has clear inputs, activities, and outputs. The cycle repeats on a quarterly cadence and on any material change to source documents or access policy.
Phase 1 — Assess: Build the Asset Register
Before a domain steward can approve any source for a Claude knowledge base, they need a complete inventory of what data assets exist in their domain and what risks each carries.
Asset Register Template

# domain-asset-register.yaml — maintained by domain steward, quarterly review

domain: "Legal"
steward: "Legal Data Steward (Maria Chen)"
last_reviewed: "2025-06-01"

assets:
  - id: "legal-contracts-repo"
    description: "Executed customer contracts in SharePoint Legal library"
    classification: "confidential"
    contains_pii: true
    pii_categories: ["contact_details", "payment_terms"]
    sensitive_categories: []       # GDPR Art. 9 categories
    retention_policy: "7 years post-contract-end"
    approved_for_claude: false     # pending legal review of SCC applicability
    approved_use_cases: []

  - id: "legal-policy-docs"
    description: "Internal legal policies and procedures (non-client-specific)"
    classification: "internal"
    contains_pii: false
    approved_for_claude: true
    approved_use_cases:
      - "legal-assistant-v1"      # HR employee policy queries
    acl:
      - role: "all-employees"     # readable by all staff
    ingest_frequency: "on-update"

  - id: "litigation-files"
    description: "Active and closed litigation case files"
    classification: "restricted"
    contains_pii: true
    approved_for_claude: false
    notes: "Legal privilege — never expose to Claude without GC written approval"
Phase 2 — Approve: Source Approval Decision

The steward reviews each candidate data source against the Claude use case's requirements and makes an explicit approve/reject/conditional-approve decision. This decision is recorded in the use-case register and referenced in the GDPR Record of Processing Activities.
Approval Decision Criteria
Criterion
Questions the Steward Asks
Outcome if Criterion Fails
Necessity
Is this data actually needed by the Claude use case? Could the use case work with less sensitive data?
Reject or require data minimisation before approval
Classification fit
Is the data classification appropriate for the target user population of the app?
Reject or require ACL restriction to appropriate roles
PII proportionality
Does including personal data provide enough benefit to justify the GDPR processing?
Reject or require pseudonymisation of PII before ingest
Freshness commitment
Will the steward commit to removing/updating this source when it becomes stale?
Conditional approval with explicit review date
Privilege / confidentiality
Is the data subject to legal professional privilege, trade secret protection, or NDA?
Reject outright — legal counsel must review separately
Source Approval Form

# source-approval-form.yaml — one per source per use case

use_case_id: "legal-assistant-v1"
source_id: "legal-policy-docs"
domain_steward: "Maria Chen"
decision_date: "2025-06-01"

decision: "approved"  # approved | rejected | conditional

conditions:
  - "Exclude any policy documents marked DRAFT from ingest"
  - "Re-approve required if document classification changes"

acl_mapping:
  permitted_roles:
    - "all-employees"
  excluded_roles:
    - "contractors"     # contractors may not see all internal policies

data_minimisation_applied: false   # no PII in policy docs — not needed

retention_override: null           # use default 90-day RAG cache retention

review_due: "2025-12-01"          # mandatory re-approval before this date
Phase 3 — Ingest: Steward Sign-off on Technical Implementation

The domain steward does not run the ingestion pipeline — the technical steward does. But the domain steward must verify that the implementation matches the approval: correct ACLs applied, correct chunks included, excluded documents not present.

# steward_ingest_verification.py
# Domain steward runs this script after each ingest to verify correctness.

import json
from pathlib import Path

def verify_ingest(
    source_id: str,
    approved_acl: list[str],
    excluded_patterns: list[str],  # e.g. ["DRAFT", "ARCHIVED"]
    chroma_collection,
) -> dict:
    results = chroma_collection.get(
        where={"source_id": source_id},
        include=["metadatas", "documents"],
    )
    violations = []

    for meta, doc in zip(results["metadatas"], results["documents"]):
        # Check ACL
        chunk_acl = json.loads(meta.get("acl", "[]"))
        if sorted(chunk_acl) != sorted(approved_acl):
            violations.append(f"ACL mismatch on chunk {meta.get('chunk_id')}: "
                               f"expected {approved_acl}, got {chunk_acl}")
        # Check excluded content
        for pattern in excluded_patterns:
            if pattern.lower() in doc.lower():
                violations.append(f"Excluded pattern '{pattern}' found in chunk {meta.get('chunk_id')}")

    return {
        "source_id": source_id,
        "total_chunks": len(results["ids"]),
        "violations": violations,
        "status": "PASS" if not violations else "FAIL",
    }
Phase 4 — Monitor: Ongoing Knowledge Base Health

Approved knowledge bases drift over time: source documents are updated or retracted, ACLs change, and usage patterns reveal unexpected data access. Domain stewards review a monthly health report generated by the technical steward.
Knowledge Base Health Metrics
Metric
Threshold
Action if Breached
Source freshness
No source older than its declared refresh frequency
Technical steward re-ingests; steward reviews if content has changed materially
Chunk count delta
> 20% change month-over-month
Steward reviews what was added/removed — unexpected growth may indicate unsanctioned sources
ACL violation count
0 per month (strict)
Immediate escalation to AI Governance Lead and DPO
Retrieval hit rate by source
No single source > 60% of all retrievals
Review whether source is appropriately scoped or crowding out other approved sources
Unapproved source detected
0 (any occurrence)
Remove immediately; investigate how it was ingested; update controls
Monthly Health Check Script
Phase 5 — Retire: Removing Sources Safely

Sources must be retired when they are retracted, superseded by updated versions, or when the use case is decommissioned. Retirement is not just deletion from the vector store — it requires updating lineage records, flagging dependent AI responses, and notifying the DPO if personal data was involved.

from datetime import datetime, timezone
import json
from pathlib import Path

RETIREMENT_LOG = Path("logs/source_retirements.jsonl")

def retire_source(
    source_id: str,
    reason: str,
    steward: str,
    collection,
    lineage_log: str = "logs/claude_lineage.jsonl",
):
    # 1. Remove from vector store
    existing = collection.get(where={"source_id": source_id})
    chunk_count = len(existing["ids"])
    if chunk_count:
        collection.delete(ids=existing["ids"])

    # 2. Find affected traces
    affected_traces = []
    for line in Path(lineage_log).read_text().splitlines():
        span = json.loads(line)
        if (span.get("name") == "claude.rag_retrieval"
                and source_id in span.get("attributes", {}).get("claude.rag.source_ids", [])):
            affected_traces.append(span["trace_id"])

    # 3. Write retirement record
    record = {
        "source_id": source_id,
        "retired_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "retired_by": steward,
        "reason": reason,
        "chunks_removed": chunk_count,
        "affected_trace_count": len(set(affected_traces)),
        "affected_trace_ids": list(set(affected_traces))[:50],
    }
    with RETIREMENT_LOG.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")

    print(f"Retired {source_id}: {chunk_count} chunks removed, "
          f"{len(set(affected_traces))} traces affected")
    return record
Common Stewardship Pitfalls
Domain stewards new to AI governance often make the same mistakes. Recognising these patterns early prevents compliance failures that are expensive to remediate.
Pitfall
What Happens
Prevention
Approving too broadly
Entire SharePoint library approved for Claude; confidential HR records retrieved by line managers
Approve at the folder/document-type level, not the library level; set explicit ACLs per role
Skipping freshness reviews
Superseded policy documents retrieved by Claude; employees receive out-of-date guidance
Set calendar reminders for review_due dates in approval forms; integrate with document management webhooks
Treating classification as permanent
Document reclassified from internal to restricted after a compliance incident, but not re-approved for Claude
Hook the knowledge base retirement workflow into the document management system's classification-change event
No test retrieval after ingest
ACL misconfiguration allows contractors to retrieve confidential content
Run steward_ingest_verification.py after every ingest; require sign-off before the app goes live
Approving use cases, not sources
Steward approves 'the HR assistant' without specifying which data sources are permitted
Require a source-level approval form for every source in every use case — one form per source per use case
Tooling Support for Stewards

Domain stewards are not engineers and should not need to query vector databases or read log files directly. A stewardship portal — even a simple one built on top of existing lineage data — dramatically reduces the effort of ongoing oversight.
Minimum Viable Stewardship Dashboard

# steward_dashboard.py — generates a simple HTML report for the domain steward # Run weekly; email to steward. from pathlib import Path import json from datetime import datetime, timezone def steward_dashboard(domain: str, source_ids: list[str], collection, lineage_log: str) -> str: rows = [] for src_id in source_ids: chunks = collection.get(where={"source_id": src_id}, include=["metadatas"]) count = len(chunks["ids"]) timestamps = [m.get("ingest_timestamp","") for m in chunks["metadatas"]] latest = max(timestamps) if timestamps else "never" rows.append(f"{src_id}{count}{latest[:10]}" f"{'OK' if count > 0 else 'MISSING'}") table_html = "\n".join(rows) return f""" Knowledge Base Health — {domain}

{domain} Domain — Knowledge Base Health Report

Generated: {datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M UTC')}

{table_html}
Source IDChunk CountLast IngestStatus
""" # Save and email html = steward_dashboard("Legal", ["legal-policy-docs"], collection, "logs/claude_lineage.jsonl") Path("reports/legal_kb_health.html").write_text(html)
Summary

Domain stewardship for Claude knowledge bases follows a five-phase lifecycle: assess (build an asset register with classifications), approve (make an explicit per-source decision with ACL mappings), ingest (verify the technical implementation matches the approval), monitor (review monthly health metrics including freshness, access patterns, and ACL violations), and retire (remove sources safely with lineage propagation and DPO notification). Each phase produces a documented output that forms part of the organisation's data governance audit trail. The domain steward is not a bottleneck — they are the single accountable owner who ensures Claude retrieves only what it is permitted to retrieve.
